title: Why Trump’s Congo Deal Is Falling Apart
gist: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns argue the Trump administration's transactional 'America first' Congo strategy — trading political support for critical-mineral access while sidelining career diplomats — produced quick wins (a DRC-Rwanda peace declaration, a wave of U.S. mining deals) but is now unraveling: Rwanda-backed M23 forces have resumed fighting, U.S. pressure has fallen asymmetrically on Kigali while Kinshasa drifts toward authoritarianism, and even a full U.S. mineral push is unlikely to dislodge China's dominance of Congolese supply chains.

themes.theme: The peace deal is collapsing on contact

insights.claim: At the December 2025 Washington ceremony meant to showcase Trump's dealmaking, Congo's Tshisekedi and Rwanda's Kagame refused to shake hands even as Trump praised their rapport, and Rwandan troops were already massing in South Kivu for a new offensive that took the town of Uvira within days.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: The signing-ceremony optics were already contradicted by facts on the ground, signaling that the underlying peace agreement lacked real buy-in from either side — a leading indicator the deal would not hold.
insights.quote: Look at how they love each other
insights.locator: When the presidents of the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Rwanda met

insights.claim: Rwanda and the M23, which are militarily superior to Congolese forces in most contested areas after inflicting heavy losses, are unlikely to cede territory the size of Maryland, yet Kinshasa's current negotiating position demands Rwanda disarm before talks even begin.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Identifies the core negotiating deadlock: Kinshasa's maximalist precondition is incompatible with Rwanda/M23's actual battlefield leverage, making renewed stalemate or escalation more likely than a durable deal.
insights.locator: Any peace deal will require compromises from both sides

insights.claim: Both the Congolese army (aided by private military companies) and the M23 have shifted increasingly to drone warfare, and in March a Kinshasa-authorized drone strike in Goma mistakenly killed a UNICEF humanitarian worker.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Signals both an escalatory shift in the conflict's technology and a rising risk of civilian and international-personnel casualties that could trigger new diplomatic incidents.
insights.quote: a drone attack in Goma, authorized by Kinshasa, mistakenly killed a UNICEF humanitarian worker
insights.locator: The Congolese army, assisted by private military companies

themes.theme: Personalist dealmaking replaces the interagency process

insights.claim: Trump named Massad Boulos — his daughter Tiffany's father-in-law, with no prior government experience — as senior adviser for Africa, and Boulos's influence over the Congo-Rwanda-M23 file derives less from his formal State Department position than from his perceived closeness to the president, echoing the pattern of U.S. envoys to Ukraine and the Middle East.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Highlights a governance pattern across the Trump administration's foreign policy — personal-relationship envoys bypassing institutional process — that makes deal durability dependent on individual access rather than career-diplomat continuity.
insights.quote: had no government experience before his appointment
insights.locator: Formal responsibility for Africa policy remains within the State Department

insights.claim: The bypass of the normal interagency process did produce results career diplomats had failed to achieve: it overcame Pentagon pushback on Rwanda sanctions (the Pentagon has strong ties to the Rwandan army) and led to a June 2025 Congo-Rwanda peace agreement and a November 2025 Qatari-brokered Congo-M23 framework.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Concedes the transactional approach's short-term effectiveness, which is the basis for the article's broader argument that Trump's method trades quick wins for weaker long-term durability.
insights.locator: The lack of policy process produced some quick results

insights.claim: In private, senior Congolese officials described their pitch to Washington simply as: 'Get Rwanda off our back, and we will give you minerals' — revealing the transactional quid pro quo underlying the entire diplomatic framework.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Cuts through the diplomatic framing to the transactional core of the deal, explaining why Kinshasa has strong incentive to keep playing to U.S. mineral interests regardless of its own domestic-governance behavior.
insights.quote: Get Rwanda off our back, and we will give you minerals
insights.locator: In private conversations, senior Congolese officials told us

insights.claim: Congo moved fast on its mineral commitments: the day after the December 2025 agreement, Gécamines and Swiss trader Mercuria announced a DFC-backed joint venture to market cobalt and copper to the U.S. and allies, and in January Gécamines exercised its right to sell 100,000 tons of Tenke Fungurume copper to the U.S. market.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Concrete, near-term evidence of U.S. mineral offtake deals materializing in Congo — a data point for tracking whether the diversification-from-China thesis is actually being executed.
insights.quote: exercise its right to purchase 100,000 tons of copper from the Tenke Fungurume mine

insights.claim: Additional U.S.-linked mineral deals followed: the Orion Critical Mineral Consortium signed a nonbinding MOU to acquire a 40% stake in Glencore's Congolese copper and cobalt assets; Virtus Minerals (founded by former U.S. military and intelligence officials) moved to take over Chemaf's assets from its Dubai owner; and Ivanhoe Mines entered advanced talks to supply the U.S. with Congolese minerals.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: A watchlist of specific corporate names and stakes for investors tracking the U.S. push into Congolese copper/cobalt supply chains — several are still nonbinding or in early talks, so execution risk remains high.
insights.quote: acquire a 40 percent stake in Glencore’s Congolese copper and cobalt assets
insights.locator: The New York–based Orion Critical Mineral Consortium

insights.claim: Congress expanded the Development Finance Corporation's maximum contingent liability cap more than threefold to $205 billion in 2025, funding projects across Angola, Gabon, Malawi, Rwanda, Zambia, and Congo, including the Lobito Corridor — an 800-mile rail and port project linking the Congo-Zambia copper/cobalt belt to the Atlantic to reduce reliance on Chinese-dominated logistics.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Identifies the DFC's massively expanded lending capacity as the primary financing vehicle for the U.S. Africa minerals strategy — a concrete policy lever with a specific dollar ceiling to track for further deployment.
insights.quote: expanded the agency’s maximum contingent liability cap more than threefold, to $205 billion

insights.claim: Even a large expansion of U.S. mining presence is unlikely to displace China's supply-chain dominance: over two-thirds of Congo's copper and cobalt exports already go to China, more than half of Africa's bauxite, manganese, tantalum, and tungsten follow the same route, and Chinese firms dominate the downstream refining and manufacturing stages as well.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Directly challenges the premise that U.S. mineral deals will meaningfully de-risk Western critical-mineral supply chains from China in the near term — a key caveat for any 'de-dollarization of critical minerals' investment thesis.
insights.quote: Over two-thirds of Congo’s copper and cobalt exports go to China
insights.locator: Washington is interested in African minerals to reduce dependence on China

insights.claim: Washington has sanctioned Rwanda's Defence Force, top officers, ex-president Joseph Kabila, and a state gold refinery, but has applied no equivalent pressure on Kinshasa — an imbalance the authors say could let Congo refuse compromise and drift toward authoritarianism.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: The article's central political-risk warning — one-sided sanctions pressure removes Kinshasa's incentive to negotiate in good faith and increases the odds of a destabilizing authoritarian turn that could jeopardize the same mineral deals Washington is courting.
insights.locator: Instead, Trump’s focus on U.S. economic interests

insights.claim: Since October 2024, Tshisekedi has signaled interest in revising or replacing Congo's constitution, potentially to seek a third term, and his coalition has escalated this push and cracked down on opposition since Washington's latest round of Rwanda sanctions.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: A specific political-risk marker — constitutional revision efforts and opposition crackdowns are concrete, trackable signals of the authoritarian drift the authors warn will follow from lopsided U.S. pressure.
insights.locator: That last danger has become increasingly apparent

insights.claim: Members of Tshisekedi's administration privately believe Trump will accept a third Tshisekedi term in exchange for mineral access and other favors, including Congo hosting migrants expelled from the United States — a first wave of which arrived in Kinshasa in April.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Suggests Kinshasa's political calculus treats U.S. mineral appetite as leverage to secure tacit U.S. acceptance of unconstitutional power retention — a governance risk directly tied to the durability of the minerals deals.
insights.locator: In private conversations, members of Tshisekedi’s administration

insights.claim: The authors recommend Washington explicitly state it opposes a third term for Tshisekedi (whose term ends in 2028) or any extralegal attempt to remain in power, using sanctions, visa restrictions, and high-level messaging to protect Congo's democracy while pressing both Kinshasa and Kigali to compromise.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: The article's explicit policy prescription — a concrete benchmark for judging whether the Trump administration course-corrects toward balanced, democracy-protective pressure versus continuing the current asymmetric approach.
insights.quote: clearly stating that it opposes a third term for Tshisekedi

themes.theme: Diplomatic capacity has been hollowed out

insights.claim: The State Department's Africa Bureau was led by a succession of acting heads for the first 16 months of the administration, and the U.S. lacks a confirmed ambassador in 39 of its 52 African embassies, including in Congo and Rwanda — undermining the government's ability to conduct complex diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Quantifies the institutional hollowing behind the article's broader critique — even where personalist dealmaking scores quick wins, the lack of career-diplomat staffing leaves policy implementation incoherent.
insights.quote: does not have an ambassador in 39 of its 52 embassies in Africa
insights.locator: The Africa Bureau was led by a succession of acting heads

insights.claim: Even as it escalated sanctions pressure on Kigali over its role in the Congo conflict, Washington separately announced up to $158 million in health-care assistance to Rwanda and signed a strategic civil-nuclear cooperation memorandum with it — a contradiction the authors cite as evidence of incoherent policy.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: A concrete example of how thin diplomatic bandwidth produces self-undermining policy — sanctions leverage on Rwanda is diluted by simultaneous cooperation agreements from other parts of the U.S. government.
insights.quote: announced a plan to provide Rwanda up to $158 million for health-care assistance and signed a memorandum on strategic civil nuclear cooperation
insights.locator: Since Rwanda escalated its meddling in Congo

insights.claim: The authors' bottom-line thesis is that without a course correction toward a longer-term, balanced strategy, the eastern Congo peace process risks collapse, U.S. mining ventures could amount to little, and Congo's president could accelerate his authoritarian turn — 'a short-term victory without a long-term strategic goal will soon feel like no victory at all.'
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: The article's capstone conclusion, framing the durability of both the peace deal and the mineral investments as contingent on a strategic shift Washington has not yet made — the single clearest signal for judging follow-on Congo coverage.
insights.quote: A short-term victory without a long-term strategic goal will soon feel like no victory at all
insights.locator: For now, despite the many ceremonies

insights.claim: By needlessly antagonizing traditional U.S. partners including European countries, South Africa, and the United Nations, Washington has made it harder to coordinate the kind of multilateral policy that has been useful in advancing past Congo peace processes.
insights.speaker: Joshua Z. Walker, Reagan Miviri, and Jason K. Stearns
insights.so_what: Warns that the transactional, go-it-alone approach carries a coordination cost with allies whose cooperation has historically been necessary for durable Congo peace efforts.
insights.locator: And by needlessly antagonizing the United States’ traditional partners
